verdicts.title: HIT REST Endpoint — Unauthenticated Visitor Tracking (Intentional Design, Signature-Gated)
verdicts.attack_vector: POST /wp-json/wp-statistics/v2/hit is intentionally public for cache-bypass tracking. It requires a valid signature: MD5(wp_salt() + json_encode([source_type, source_id])). Without the server-side wp_salt the signature cannot be forged. The endpoint only registers when Option::get('use_cache_plugin') is true. All data is stored via $wpdb->insert() — no raw SQL.
verdicts.evidence: 'permission_callback' => function($request){ return $this->checkSignature($request); } — checkSignature returns WP_Error 403 when signature check fails. Signature is md5(wp_salt().json_encode($payload)) in Utils/Signature.php.
verdicts.remediation: No action needed. Keep Request Signatures enabled (default). If signatures are disabled via filter, the endpoint accepts unauthenticated data — document this risk clearly.

verdicts.title: BaseRestAPI Default permissionCallback Returns true — Insecure Default for Subclasses
verdicts.attack_vector: src/Abstracts/BaseRestAPI.php line 85 defines permissionCallback() returning true unconditionally. Any future endpoint subclassing BaseRestAPI without overriding permissionCallback will be open to unauthenticated callers. The two known subclasses (Hit, MetaBox) both override this correctly, but the dangerous default creates a trap for add-on authors.
verdicts.auth_required: none (for any future subclass that omits the override)
verdicts.remediation: Either (a) make permissionCallback abstract so subclasses are forced to implement it explicitly, or (b) change the default to return is_user_logged_in() and require public endpoints to override with return true. Add a PHPDoc warning on the current signature.

verdicts.title: Metabox REST Endpoint — Properly Authenticated (False Positive)
verdicts.auth_required: subscriber+ with read_capability (defaults to manage_options = admin only)
verdicts.evidence: 'permission_callback' => function($request){ $user = wp_get_current_user(); if($user->ID == 0){ return false; } return current_user_can(Option::get('read_capability','manage_options')); }

verdicts.title: AJAX nopriv Handlers — All Nonce/Capability Gated (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: class-wp-statistics-admin-ajax.php registers 6 actions all with $isPublic=false — none exposed as nopriv. src/Components/Ajax.php is called with public=true (default) for: custom_event, dismiss_notification, update_notifications_status, option_updater, and metabox get_data. Each handler independently verifies a nonce (wp_verify_nonce or check_ajax_referer) which requires an active authenticated WordPress session to generate.
verdicts.auth_required: subscriber+ (nonce ties action to logged-in session)
verdicts.remediation: Defence-in-depth: change Ajax::register() default $public parameter to false so new handlers must opt-in to nopriv exposure.

verdicts.title: wp-async-request Nopriv AJAX — Nonce Protected, Internal Use Only (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: includes/libraries/wp-background-processing/wp-async-request.php registers wp_ajax_nopriv_{identifier}. maybe_handle() calls check_ajax_referer($this->identifier,'nonce') before processing. The nonce is generated server-side (wp_create_nonce($this->identifier)) and sent only in internal wp_remote_post() calls with the site's own session cookies — never exposed to external clients.
verdicts.evidence: Line 172: check_ajax_referer($this->identifier,'nonce'). Dispatch sends nonce via wp_remote_post with $_COOKIE. Background process version also calls check_ajax_referer at line 337.
verdicts.remediation: No action needed. Standard WP background processing pattern.

verdicts.title: Helper::getConditionSQL — Unparameterized SQL Builder (Latent SQLi Risk)
verdicts.attack_vector: Helper::getConditionSQL() builds WHERE clauses by directly concatenating $params['key'] (column name), $params['compare'] (SQL operator), and $params['value']/$params['from']/$params['to'] (values) — none via $wpdb->prepare(). The only in-tree caller is Visitor::Count() which is only invoked with internal args, but the method is public API and could be called by add-ons/third-party code. A vulnerable caller passing user-controlled values would allow full SQL injection including UNION SELECT, time-based blind, and stacked queries.
verdicts.auth_required: depends on caller (potentially none if an add-on exposes it)
verdicts.evidence: sql .= $params['key'].' '.$params['compare'].' '.(is_numeric($params['value']) ? $params['value'] : "'".$params['value']."'"); — is_numeric() check on value is not SQL-injection-safe; key and compare are unvalidated.
verdicts.remediation: Rewrite getConditionSQL to: (1) validate 'key' against a whitelist of known column names, (2) validate 'compare' against allowed operators, (3) use $wpdb->prepare() for values. Alternatively deprecate the function — the plugin's modern models use a query-builder pattern that is safe.

verdicts.title: DB::ExistTable / getTableInformation — Raw SQL on Plugin-Controlled Table Names (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: ExistTable($tbl_name) uses SHOW TABLES LIKE '$tbl_name' and getTableInformation uses SHOW TABLE STATUS LIKE '$table_name' without prepare(). All callers (Updater.php, Install.php) pass values from DB::table() which returns plugin-controlled strings (e.g. wp_statistics_visitor). No code path allows external user input to reach these methods.
verdicts.evidence: All ExistTable callers in Updater.php pass e.g. $searchTable = DB::table('search'); — fully hardcoded. No $_GET/$_POST/$_REQUEST data flows to these methods.
verdicts.remediation: Low-priority hardening: use $wpdb->prepare('SHOW TABLES LIKE %s',$tbl_name) for phpcs compliance.

verdicts.title: Referred::getList Date/Limit Concatenation — No Active Exploit Path (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: Referred::getList() concatenates $args['from'], $args['to'] into a BETWEEN clause and $args['min']/$args['limit'] directly into SQL without prepare(). The only caller in the plugin (FilterManager.php:216) passes array('min'=>50,'limit'=>300) — static integers, no user-supplied 'from' or 'to'. No admin form or request parameter flows into this function.
verdicts.evidence: FilterManager.php:216: Referred::getList(array('min'=>50,'limit'=>300)); — 'from' and 'to' keys absent. The $where variable is empty string, so the BETWEEN clause is never constructed.
verdicts.remediation: Hardening: apply $wpdb->prepare() or intval()/sanitize_text_field() to all dynamic args in getList() to protect future callers.

verdicts.title: Stored XSS via Visitor Analytics Data — Properly Escaped at Output (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: Visitor-controlled data (referrer URL, browser agent, IP, platform) is stored from unauthenticated hits and displayed in admin analytics views. All admin templates use esc_html(), esc_url(), esc_attr() at point of output. Referrer href attributes use esc_url(). Source-name/channel text uses esc_html(). The html_sanitize_referrer() method strips javascript: and data: URI schemes. No unescaped echo of visitor-controlled fields found in any template.
verdicts.evidence: <a href='<?php echo esc_url($referrer->getReferrer()) ?>' title='<?php echo esc_html($referrer->getRawReferrer()) ?>'>. Referred::html_sanitize_referrer() blocks javascript: and data: schemes. get_referrer_link() applies esc_url() to href.

verdicts.title: Helper::validateHitRequest Injection Blocklist — Bypassable, Not a Security Control
verdicts.attack_vector: injectionPatterns() in Helper provides a regex blocklist applied to page_uri, search_query, and referred in the HIT endpoint. The list is not exhaustive (misses hex encoding, stacked queries, second-order injection, and many XSS variants). However, actual data storage uses $wpdb->insert() (parameterised) and output uses esc_html()/esc_url(), so the blocklist is defence-in-depth anti-spam rather than the primary security control. The concern is if developers rely on this blocklist for other purposes.
verdicts.evidence: Patterns cover UNION SELECT, INSERT INTO, DELETE, DROP, ALTER, OR 1=1, sleep(, benchmark(, <script>, on* handlers — but not hex-encoded payloads (e.g. 0x53454c454354), second-order injection, or less common vectors. The filter is also overridable via apply_filters('wp_statistics_injection_patterns',...).
verdicts.remediation: Document that injectionPatterns() is an anti-spam/rate-limit control, not a security sanitiser. Confirm all data handling downstream uses parameterised queries and proper output escaping (currently true). Remove or clarify the filter hook to avoid false reliance.

verdicts.title: Helper Static WordPress Core Queries — No User Input, No Risk (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: Lines 1230, 1264, 1279, 1280, 1312, 1313, 1345, 1346 run SELECT queries on $wpdb->comments, $wpdb->posts, $wpdb->users without $wpdb->prepare(). All queries are fully static — no WHERE clause accepts user input. Equivalent to hardcoded strings.
verdicts.auth_required: admin (called from admin dashboard summary widgets)
verdicts.evidence: $wpdb->get_var("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {$wpdb->comments} WHERE comment_approved = '1'") — {$wpdb->comments} is a WordPress internal property set at init, not user-controllable.
verdicts.remediation: Cosmetic: use $wpdb->prepare() for phpcs compliance. No security impact.

key_observations: No exploitable SQL injection found. The confirmed SQLi-pattern code (getConditionSQL) has no user-controlled caller in the current plugin version.
No stored XSS found. All visitor-supplied data is correctly escaped at output using WordPress escaping functions.
All AJAX nopriv handlers require valid nonces tied to authenticated sessions. The use of Ajax::register() with public=true default is a code quality concern, not an active vulnerability.

The HIT REST endpoint is intentionally public but protected by a server-secret signature (md5(wp_salt()+payload)). Disabling signatures via the wp_statistics_request_signature_enabled filter would open it to stat-spam but not SQLi.

The only confirmed finding (WPS-002) is a latent risk from the BaseRestAPI insecure default permissionCallback. Current endpoints override it correctly but future add-ons could be caught by this trap.
The 58 Semgrep findings are predominantly false positives from static analysis tools that cannot trace data flow through WordPress's parameterised query helpers and escaping functions.
